channel.title: Gaming on Plot Twist Daily
channel.description: Recent content in Gaming on Plot Twist Daily

item.title: The Gaming Industry's Dirty Secret About Crunch
item.author: arty.craftson@pottersquill.com (Arty Craftson)
item.description:
Another AAA game studio just announced delays. They blamed “quality concerns.” But everyone knows the real reason.

Context

Game development has a problem: crunch culture.

Developers routinely work 60-80 hour weeks for months before a game ships. It’s called “crunch” and it’s been standard practice for decades.

Recent examples:

  • Cyberpunk 2077: Developers worked 6-day weeks for months
  • Red Dead Redemption 2: 100-hour weeks reported
  • The Last of Us Part II: “Final sprint” lasted 6 months

The result? Burned-out developers, buggy games, and delayed releases.

item.title: Lenovo's Modular Gaming Laptop Is Wild—and It Just Might Work
item.author: arty.craftson@pottersquill.com (Arty Craftson)
item.description:
Lenovo’s Modular Gaming Laptop Is Wild—and It Just Might Work

Lenovo brought a robot arm to MWC. It has puppy dog eyes.

No, really. The arm—designed to hold a tablet while you work—has two LED lights positioned to look like eyes. They blink. They follow your movement. It’s adorable. It’s unnecessary. It’s exactly the kind of weird that modular hardware needs.

The Modular Dream

PC gamers have wanted modular laptops for a decade. Desktop users can swap GPUs, CPUs, RAM on a whim. Laptop users get soldered components and planned obsolescence.
